summary: David Hume explores the different species of philosophy, advocating for a balance between profound inquiry and practical life. He establishes the foundation of his empiricism by dividing perceptions into lively 'impressions' and faint 'ideas', arguing that all ideas are ultimately derived from precedent impressions.

principles.statement: All ideas are copies of impressions.
principles.rationale: When we analyze our thoughts, even complex or sublime ones, they resolve into simple ideas copied from precedent feelings. Furthermore, if a person lacks a sense organ, they are entirely incapable of forming the corresponding ideas.
principles.source_quote: all our ideas or more feeble perceptions are copies of our impressions or more lively ones.

principles.statement: Accurate and abstruse philosophy is necessary to support easy and humane philosophy.
principles.rationale: Just as an anatomist's knowledge of internal structure helps a painter draw a beautiful figure, an accurate knowledge of the mind's operations improves polite letters, practical arts, and delicate sentiments.
principles.source_quote: Accuracy is, in every case, advantageous to beauty, and just reasoning to delicate sentiment.

frameworks.name: The Impression Test for Meaning
frameworks.when_to_apply: When suspecting that a philosophical term is being used without a clear meaning or determinate idea.
steps: Identify the philosophical term or abstract idea in question.
Ask: 'from what impression is that supposed idea derived?'
If it is impossible to assign any precedent impression (sensation or feeling), conclude the term is meaningless and reject it.
frameworks.source_quote: When we entertain, therefore, any suspicion, that a philosophical term is employed without any meaning or idea (as is but too frequent), we need but enquire, from what impression is that supposed idea derived?

mental_models.name: Mental Geography
mental_models.description: Viewing the study of the mind as a mapping exercise, where the goal is to delineate, separate, and class the distinct parts, powers, and operations of the mind, much like mapping physical territories or planetary systems.
mental_models.example: Distinguishing between the will and understanding, or the imagination and passions, to correct the seeming disorder of mental operations when made the object of reflection.

mental_models.name: The Anatomist and the Painter
mental_models.description: A metaphor illustrating the relationship between abstruse philosophy (the anatomist) and easy, practical philosophy (the painter). The anatomist studies the hidden, sometimes disagreeable internal structures, which is necessary for the painter to accurately and beautifully depict the outward form.
mental_models.example: A painter employing rich colors to paint Venus must still carry attention to the inward structure of the human body, just as a moralist needs accurate knowledge of the internal fabric of the understanding to describe life and manners.

heuristics: Be a philosopher; but, amidst all your philosophy, be still a man.
When a term seems meaningless, ask: from what impression is it derived?

signature_quotes.text: Be a philosopher; but, amidst all your philosophy, be still a man.
signature_quotes.context: Advising a mixed kind of life that balances profound research with action and society.

signature_quotes.text: The most lively thought is still inferior to the dullest sensation.
signature_quotes.context: Distinguishing between the force and vivacity of ideas versus impressions.

signature_quotes.text: Accuracy is, in every case, advantageous to beauty, and just reasoning to delicate sentiment.
signature_quotes.context: Defending the utility of accurate, abstruse philosophy in supporting the practical arts.

signature_quotes.text: All our ideas or more feeble perceptions are copies of our impressions or more lively ones.
signature_quotes.context: Stating the core principle of his empiricist theory of mind.

anti_patterns.description: Engaging in false or adulterate metaphysics driven by human vanity or popular superstition.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It attempts to penetrate subjects utterly inaccessible to human understanding, serving only as a shelter to superstition and a cover to absurdity and error.

anti_patterns.description: Rejecting all profound reasonings or metaphysics entirely because they are difficult or sometimes misused.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It leaves superstition in possession of its retreat. The only way to free learning from abstruse questions is to seriously inquire into the nature of human understanding and map its actual limits.
